title: Day 3 — Model types & frontier labs
summary: Read Step 1 → 14 in order. §1 model types · §2 frontier labs · §3 product demos (deep research, agents, Claude Code).

sections.heading: What you already did & today's goal
sections.remember: You can call **frontier models** (OpenAI, Gemini, **Llama** via API/Ollama) and **summarize webpages** — today you learn **four model types** and **chat vs reasoning**.
points: Progression: first API contact → local models → compare **what each model type is trained for**.
By end of day: know **base**, **chat/instruct**, **reasoning**, and **hybrid** models — strengths and trade-offs.

sections.heading: Base model
sections.remember: A **base model** only does **next-token prediction** on a sequence — complete the text, not chat.
points: Input = token sequence → output = most likely **next tokens** (repeat to generate length).
Rare in products you use directly; you **will** use one later when **fine-tuning** a new skill from scratch.
Real-world analogy: phone **predictive text** — each word you pick extends the sequence; the model suggests what comes next.
Pre-**ChatGPT** era (e.g. **GPT-3** as base): power users prompted with patterns like `Q: … A: …` then `Q: your question` + `A:` to force Q&A style — hacky, not native chat.

sections.heading: Chat / Instruct model
sections.remember: **Chat** (or **Instruct**) models are fine-tuned on **message → response** pairs — roles like **system**, **user**, **assistant**.
points: Training adds dialog structure on top of the base model (e.g. **RLHF** — reinforcement learning from human feedback) → **ChatGPT**.
**System prompt** — overall instructions for the whole conversation.
**User** / **assistant** turns alternate — this is the API **`messages`** format you use in labs.
Prompt trick (no extra training): **chain-of-thought** — e.g. end with “think step by step” so the model emits a more methodical token sequence.

sections.heading: Reasoning model
sections.remember: A **reasoning** (thinking) model is trained to emit a **reasoning trace** first, then the final answer.
points: Evolution: chain-of-thought prompting → train on examples that **show thinking steps** before the conclusion.
You see this in products (e.g. **OpenAI o-series** / “thinking” UI) — internal tokens for reasoning, then user-facing reply.
Generally **stronger on puzzles and hard problems**; uses more tokens (thinking + answer) → **slower, pricier**.
Anecdote: can feel **cold or over-analytical** for casual creative writing vs chat models — test yourself.

sections.heading: Hybrid model, reasoning budget & budget forcing
sections.remember: **Hybrid** models choose how much to reason per request; **reasoning effort** (budget) can be tuned — including hacky **budget forcing**.
points: **Hybrid** — e.g. **GPT-5**, **Gemini 2.5 Pro**: simple “hi” → little reasoning; hard puzzle → more reasoning.
**Reasoning budget** / **reasoning effort** — API or product knob for how long the model thinks.
**Budget forcing** (S1 paper, Jan 2025): inject **`wait`** into the **thinking trace** so the model continues reflecting (“wait, let me reconsider”) — surprisingly effective.
Higher reasoning budget → usually better **benchmarks**; not always best for latency, cost, or creative tone.

sections.heading: Which type when?
sections.remember: Pick the type for the **task**: fine-tune from **base**; fast dialog → **chat**; hard logic → **reasoning**; mixed → **hybrid**.
points: **Base** — starting point for **fine-tuning** a new behavior (not locked into chat/reasoning format).
**Chat** — fast, cheaper, interactive apps; often preferred for **creative / fluent** copy (informal consensus).
**Reasoning** — math, coding puzzles, multi-step problems when you accept latency and token cost.
**Hybrid** — default for modern frontier APIs when you want the model to auto-scale thinking.

right.label: Chat (interactive / creative)
lines: Better for chat UX & some writing

sections.heading: Frontier & foundation models — the major labs
sections.remember: **Frontier** / **foundation model** = large general-purpose LLM from a major lab; terms are often used interchangeably (no strict official definition).
points: **OpenAI** — **GPT-5** (hybrid); **o-series** (reasoning); **GPT 4.1** (pure **chat**, often faster than GPT-5 even at low reasoning); **OSS** (open-weight). Chat product: **ChatGPT**.
**Anthropic** — **Claude** sizes: **Haiku** (small), **Sonnet** (mid, common in course), **Opus** (large). Chat: Claude app.
**Google** — **Gemini** (e.g. 2.5; newer versions over time). Chat: **Gemini**.
**xAI** — **Grok** model + Grok chat (spelled with **k**, not Groq the inference company).
**DeepSeek** — Chinese lab; standout because flagship models are **open source**; chat: **DeepSeek**.

sections.heading: What frontier models do well
sections.remember: Strong at **synthesis**, **structured answers**, **content drafts**, and **coding** — especially when a skilled human supervises.
points: **Summarize & synthesize** — e.g. webpage summaries from earlier labs; long, structured Q&A and pros/cons.
**Generate content** — emails, presentations, project outlines; good starting point for new ideas.
**Code** — write, test, fix in loops; largely replaced Stack Overflow for many developers.
Often fixes hard bugs quickly with clear explanations — when the problem is in their knowledge.

sections.heading: Rough edges — cutoff, hallucination, tooling
sections.remember: Models predict **plausible** next tokens — so they can be **wrong but confident**; training has a **knowledge cutoff** unless extra tools are added.
points: **Knowledge gaps** — expert in some domains, weak or outdated in others (may insist on old model names like GPT-3.5).
**Knowledge cutoff** — training data ends at a date; no built-in knowledge of events after that.
**Web search in ChatGPT** = **product engineering** (tools/RAG), not the raw LLM weights knowing the live web.
**Hallucination** — confident false outputs; dangerous for **juniors** who cannot spot bad code; **seniors** get more value because they review and challenge.

sections.heading: Supervision — root cause beats band-aids
sections.remember: Treat the LLM like a tireless **junior analyst**: you must review, keep it on track, and fix wrong assumptions — especially in coding.
points: LLMs often **patch forward** instead of stepping back to the real root cause.

**Example (course story):** Hugging Face call used **base** model name instead of **instruct/chat** variant → chat `system`/`user` format failed → assistant LLM generated pages of “convert to chat” code instead of fixing the **model id**.
Lesson: wrong model type (Step 2–3) looks like a hard problem; fix the name first.
Your job as **AI engineer**: supervise outputs; validate; don’t ship auto-generated tangents blindly.

sections.heading: Deep Research (agentic pattern)
sections.remember: **Deep Research** = the product runs many steps for you (read sources, synthesize) — you feel **autonomy**: work continues while you do something else.
points: ChatGPT **Deep Research** browses many sources (e.g. 12+), cites links (TechCrunch, etc.), outputs a long **markdown** report.
Early **agentic** use case: not one prompt → one reply; a **workflow** runs in the background.
Output may reflect **bias in the task** (e.g. report ranked tech AI companies, not every commercial user of LLMs).
Still verify claims — same supervision mindset as Step 10.

sections.heading: Multimodal — image generation
sections.remember: **Multimodal** products also **generate images** (separate from text-only chat) — quality and creativity vary by model (e.g. **DALL·E 3** vs newer image models).
points: Demo: absurd prompt (“rainbows… Hawaii to 17”) → image; may be **literal** unless you prompt for style.
Runs **in parallel** with Deep Research — multiple chats/tasks at once.
Familiar tools: **GPT** image gen, **Midjourney**, etc. — same frontier ecosystem, different modality.

sections.heading: Agent mode — browser & real-world actions
sections.remember: **Agent mode** goes further: controls a **browser**, searches the web, checks sites, and can offer to **complete actions** (e.g. book a table) — not just text.
points: Example task: NYC restaurant, **British** food, **banoffee pie** on menu, **9 p.m.** availability — agent navigates sites (e.g. **Resy**), moves the mouse, verifies menu and slots.
Uses **tools** behind the chat UI (browser, search) — stronger “something is working for me” than chat alone.
Can end with: “I can **book** this for you” — human must approve before real-world commits.
Failures happen (cookies, wrong page) — agent recovers and continues; still supervise outcomes.

sections.heading: Claude Code — coding agent in your repo
sections.remember: **Claude Code** = **Claude Sonnet** (frontier model) + **agent loop** on your filesystem — reads notebooks, writes runnable code (e.g. **`solution.py`** with **uv**).
points: Demo: read `week1/day1` + `day2` notebooks → implement day 2 challenge as **`solution.py`** using **Ollama/Llama** (local base URL) instead of OpenAI.
One-shot style: understands scraper + summarizer pattern, **messages**, snarky system prompt — you run `uv run solution.py` with a URL.
Same theme as course labs, but the **agent** plans, edits files, and iterates — preview of **agentic AI** you build later in the course.
Lesson: powerful for seniors who review; same risks as Step 10 if you accept code blindly.

cheatSheet: S2 — Frontier labs · strengths · limits · supervise
S3 — Deep Research = multi-step cited reports
S3 — Agent mode = browser + tools + optional actions
S3 — Claude Code = repo-aware coding agent (Sonnet + files)
